feedback.text: The contribution of the Central European Lawyers Initiative (CELI) is provided in the attached document.

section_path: Digital Fairness Act Call for Evidence
Overview
paragraphs.text: Addictive platform design features such as autoplay, constant scroll and algorithmic recommender systems are driving societal harms, in particular for children, for example through excessive screen time, dissemination of harmful material, radicalisation and extremism. As a secondary effect, these design features also contribute to overburdening our networks with poor quality and unsolicited traffic, consuming valuable, finite network resources.

section_path: Digital Fairness Act Call for Evidence
Overview
paragraphs.text: As part of our wider push for a Responsible Use of networks, Vodafone supports the EC in bringing forward new rules under the DFA to curb the deployment of addictive design features; we support an outright ban on children's exposure to addictive design features and limitations on how and where these features can be deployed on services used by adults.

section_path: Digital Fairness Act Call for Evidence
Overview
paragraphs.text: The DFA needs to be carefully targeted to ensure that it does not unnecessarily duplicate existing consumer protection rules. Telecoms operators are already subject to onerous horizontal and sectoral rules in relation to our customers; any new requirements should be based on a thorough gap analysis an economic impact assessment to avoid unnecessary duplication and overburdening providers at a time when the EU is focused on reducing barriers and red tape for business.

section_path: Digital Fairness Act Call for Evidence
Reflections on Digital Fairness Fitness Check
paragraphs.text: The Fitness Check notes that the current EU legal framework is not equipped to address the issues raised by addictive interfaces. In particular, the Commission acknowledges the need for regulatory intervention to address harmful practices employed by the OTT's to manipulate or 'lock-in' consumers, including dark patterns, autoplay and auto-scrolling features.

section_path: Digital Fairness Act Call for Evidence
Reflections on Digital Fairness Fitness Check
paragraphs.text: The Fitness Check highlights the negative impact of an array or online tools and techniques on consumer welfare, including deceptive or addictive interface designs and functionalities, personalised practices targeting vulnerabilities, difficulties with the cancellation and renewal of digital subscriptions, forced acceptance of unfair contract terms and challenges associated with social media commerce.

section_path: Digital Fairness Act Call for Evidence
Reflections on Digital Fairness Fitness Check
paragraphs.text: Consumer protection is a key foundation and pillar of the EU, with end-users in Member States having some of the biggest protections in the world. However, existing legislation (DSA/DMA/EMFA) only partially tackle dark behaviours and are limited to platforms with over

section_path: Digital Fairness Act Call for Evidence
Reflections on Digital Fairness Fitness Check
paragraphs.text: 45M users, therefore further protections under the DFA would positively impact society by ensuring end-users are protected from dark patterns and addictive designs on all platforms and services. Consumers would also benefit from higher transparency with marketing practices, including tighter regulation on influencer marketing.

section_path: Digital Fairness Act Call for Evidence
Targeted action on addictive design features
paragraphs.text: Addictive platform design features such as autoplay, constant scroll and algorithmic recommender systems are driving societal harms, for children, for example through excessive screen time, dissemination of harmful material, radicalisation and extremism. This is not a fringe problem. A 2019 study by the European Parliament Research Service indicated that digital addiction affects millions of EU consumers. A more recent BEUC study showed that 83% of consumers report spending more time on social media than they intended. The term 'doomscrolling' has entered popular vernacular. This issue is likely to compound as advancements in AI spur increasingly personalised and targeted content, thus driving further engagement.

section_path: Digital Fairness Act Call for Evidence
Targeted action on addictive design features
paragraphs.text: This issue is compounded by opaque algorithms which have been found to push the consumption of content that promotes highly harmful material, such as content related to self-harm, suicide, 'fake news', eating disorders, misogyny, racism, and terrorism. In many cases, end-users do not even intend to access this kind of content. A recent study in the UK revealed that 59% of boys who have accessed misogynistic content came to it through unrelated searches but were pushed to this harmful content through AI algorithms.

section_path: Digital Fairness Act Call for Evidence
Targeted action on addictive design features
paragraphs.text: Despite this the Digital Services Act (DSA) and AI Act, current EU and UK frameworks do not explicitly regulate addictive design features. Key gaps include: i) Absence of a legal definition for "addictive design" in consumer protection law, ii) no legally binding requirement to disable addictive features by default, iii) lack of harmonized standards for age-appropriate design and iv) insufficient enforcement mechanisms for ethical design principles.

section_path: Digital Fairness Act Call for Evidence
Targeted action on addictive design features
paragraphs.text: Vodafone supports the EC in bringing forward new rules to curb the deployment of addictive design features; we support an outright ban on children's exposure to addictive design features and limitations on how and where these features can be deployed on services used by adults. As a baseline requirement, these features should be offered to adult users on an "opt out" basis, with users able to easily deactivate addictive design features via their account settings.

section_path: Digital Fairness Act Call for Evidence
Simplification & harmonisation
paragraphs.text: The EU wants to create a Digital Single Market, but right now, the rules for protecting consumers let each country make their own changes. This makes it hard for European businesses to work smoothly across borders. The EU Commission should focus on making these rules simpler and the same everywhere, including the DFA. This will help European

section_path: Digital Fairness Act Call for Evidence
Simplification & harmonisation
Digital Fairness Act Call for Evidence
Simplification & harmonisation
Digital Fairness Act Call for Evidence
Simplification & harmonisation
paragraphs.text: businesses compete better and be stronger financially, while still keeping a high level of consumer protection.

section_path: Digital Fairness Act Call for Evidence
Level playing field
paragraphs.text: The EU's consumer protection rules for telecommunications are complex and work alongside general consumer regulations. To ensure trust and fairness in the Digital Single Market, all providers should follow the same harmonized rules. Simplifying these regulations would reduce costs, lower legal uncertainty, and increase transparency. New measures should target those causing potential consumer harm, as seen in the Digital Service Act.
